info.topictitle: B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
info.parenttitle: B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement

markdown:
# Movement and Rotation in Planes and Axes


<definition term="Plane of movement ">A **plane of movement** is an **imaginary flat surface** that divides the body into sections and along which movements occur.</definition>

1. Movements occur within a plane and are controlled by the **muscles, joints, and nervous system**. 
2. Each plane allows specific types of movement, and understanding them is crucial for analysing human motion in sports and exercise.

<definition term="Anatomical Plane">An imaginary flat surface used to divide the body for anatomical study and movement analysis. The main anatomical planes are: **Sagittal Plane, Frontal (Coronal) Plane and Transverse Plane**</definition>

<callout type="analogy">* The body can be visualised as moving between three sheets of glass, one running from front to back, one from side to side, and one cutting horizontally. 
* Each movement must follow the direction allowed by the glass.</callout>

## The Three Anatomical Planes


1. The human body moves in t**hree anatomical planes: sagittal, frontal, and transverse**. 
2. Each plane allows different types of movement.

![Anatomical planes](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/89173d509c458f070ac1fbbe2e2873f0ca6a1f27-1760x2267.png)



### 1.  Sagittal Plane


1. The **sagittal plane** divides the body into **left and right halves**. 
2. Movements in this plane occur **forward and backward** and involve changes in **joint angles**.

<callout type="example">In a squat:

* Knee flexion during descent
* Hip flexion as you lower
* Both knee and hip extension as you rise
* All movement occurs in the sagittal plane</callout>

#### Common Movements in the Sagittal Plane


1. **Flexion: **Decreasing the angle between two bones (e.g., bending the knee in a squat).
2. **Extension:** Increasing the angle between two bones (e.g., straightening the knee in a squat).
3. **Dorsiflexion:** Lifting the toes towards the shin (e.g., during the back phase of a running stride).
4. **Plantarflexion:** Pointing the toes downward (e.g., pushing off in a sprint start).

<callout type="example">**Examples in Sports:**

* Running: The legs and arms move **forward and backward** in the sagittal plane.
* Cycling: The knees flex and extend during each pedal stroke.
* Weightlifting: Movements such as deadlifts, lunges, and squats occur primarily in this plane.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* Flexion decreases the joint angle, while extension increases it. 
* A simple rule: **Flexion makes the joint angle smaller, extension makes it larger**.</callout>

<callout type="tip">If a movement occurs in the **sagittal plane**, the **axis of rotation is perpendicular to it, **in this case, the **frontal axis**.</callout>

### 2.  Frontal Plane


1. The **frontal plane** divides the body into **front (anterior) and back (posterior) halves**. 
2. Movements in this plane occur **side-to-side**.

#### Common Movements in the Frontal Plane


1. **Abduction:** Moving a body part **away** from the midline (e.g., raising the arms in a lateral raise).
2. **Adduction:** Moving a body part **toward** the midline (e.g., bringing the arms back down in a lateral raise).
3. **Inversion:** Turning the sole of the foot **inward** (e.g., rolling an ankle).
4. **Eversion:** Turning the sole of the foot **outward**.
5. **Elevation:** Raising a body part (e.g., shrugging the shoulders).
6. **Depression:** Lowering a body part (e.g., dropping the shoulders).

<callout type="example">**Examples in Sports:**

* Jumping Jacks: The arms and legs move in the **frontal plane**.
* Side Lunges: Movement occurs **side-to-side**.
* Defense in Basketball: Lateral shuffling is an example of movement in the **frontal plane**.</callout>

<callout type="tip">A good way to remember this plane is to consider standing between **two glass walls, **you can move **side-to-side**, but not forward or backward.</callout>

### 3. Transverse Plane


1. The **transverse plane** divides the body into **top (superior) and bottom (inferior) halves**. 
2. Movements in this plane involve **rotation**.

![Transverse plane](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/8d2a9d9af5198710295fd09f29bcfd88c6f48441-1024x768.png)

#### Common Movements in the Transverse Plane


1. **Rotation:** Twisting a body part around its axis (e.g., turning the head side to side).
2. **Pronation:** Rotating the forearm so the palm faces **downward**.
3. **Supination:** Rotating the forearm so the palm faces **upward**.
4. **Horizontal Flexion:** Moving the arm across the body at shoulder level (e.g., chest fly).
5. **Horizontal Extension: **Moving the arm away from the body at shoulder level.

<callout type="analogy">1. The transverse plane is like a tabletop slicing through the waist. 
2. Movements in this plane involve rotation, such as turning your head or twisting your torso.</callout>

<callout type="example">**Examples in Sports:**

* Golf Swing: The torso **rotates** in the transverse plane.
* Baseball Pitch: The **shoulders and torso** rotate.
* Tennis Forehand: The **hips, torso, and shoulders** rotate in the transverse plane.</callout>

<callout type="warning">Movement occurs in a plane and rotation occurs around an axis. A door moves in a plane and rotates around its hinges, which act as the axis.

##</callout>

![Types of Body Movements](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/2d3c310752998e39639b89d4dea9bb2564d5636d-1200x960.jpg)

## Axes of Rotation


<definition term="Axis of rotation">An **axis of rotation** is an **imaginary line** around which movement occurs.</definition>

Each movement in a plane occurs around an axis that is **perpendicular** to that plane.

<callout type="analogy">* A door hinge provides a useful analogy for an axis of rotation.
* The door moves around the **hinge**, just like body parts move around an axis.</callout>

![Axes of Rotation](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ce00d23d711f00bbb280851a457b9dfeb7e56c06-748x729.jpg)

### 1.  Sagittal Axis (Anteroposterior Axis)


1. Runs **front to back** through the body.
2. **Perpendicular to the frontal plane.**
3. Movements around this axis include **abduction and adduction**.

<callout type="example">* A **cartwheel** occurs around the sagittal axis.
* A **side lunge** moves around the sagittal axis.
* A **jumping jack** rotates around this axis.</callout>

<callout type="tip">Many students assume the **sagittal axis** is related to the sagittal plane, but it is actually **perpendicular to the frontal plane** and controls **side-to-side movements**.</callout>

### 2.  Frontal Axis (Mediolateral Axis)


1. Runs **side to side** through the body.
2. **Perpendicular to the sagittal plane.**
3. Movements around this axis include **flexion and extension**.

<callout type="example">* A **somersault** occurs around the frontal axis.
* A **bicep curl** involves rotation around the frontal axis.
* A **squat** moves around the frontal axis.</callout>

<callout type="note">Because this axis runs side to side, it is associated with forward and backward movement.</callout>

### 3.  Longitudinal Axis (Vertical Axis)


1. Runs **top to bottom** through the body.
2. **Perpendicular to the transverse plane.**
3. Movements around this axis involve **rotation**.

<callout type="example">* A **ballet pirouette** occurs around the vertical axis.
* A **discus throw** rotates around the vertical axis.
* A **golf swing** moves around the vertical axis.</callout>

<callout type="analogy">Think of **supination **as holding a bowl of soup with your palm up.</callout>

<callout type="warning">Movement occurs in a plane, while rotation occurs around an axis.

* Movement happens **in a plane**.
* Rotation happens **around an axis**.</callout>

## Types of Movements and Their Planes


### Types of Movements in the Sagittal Plane


#### 1. Flexion


<definition term="Flexion">A movement that **decreases the angle** between two bones at a joint.</definition>

1. Typically moves a limb **forward** (except for the knee, which moves backward).
2. Involves **muscle contraction** that brings bones closer together.

**Common movements with flexion: **

1. **Elbow Flexion:** Bending the arm during a bicep curl.
2. **Knee Flexion:** Bending the knee during a squat or leg curl exercise.
3. **Hip Flexion:** Lifting the knee toward the chest (e.g., high knees in sprinting).
4. **Neck Flexion:** Tucking the chin toward the chest.

<callout type="example">* A gymnast performing a tuck jump – The knees flex to pull the legs up.
* A soccer player preparing to kick – The knee flexes before striking the ball.
* A sprinter starting from the blocks – The rear leg undergoes knee flexion before pushing off.</callout>

<callout type="tip">**Flexion **can be remembered as **a movement that makes a joint "smaller" or more "closed" (reducing the angle between bones).**</callout>

<callout type="warning">Some students think **flexion always moves a limb forward**, but at the **knee joint, flexion moves the lower leg backward**.</callout>

#### 2. Extension


<definition term="Extension">A movement that **increases the angle** between two bones at a joint.</definition>

1. Typically moves a limb **backward** (except for the knee, which moves forward).
2. Involves **muscle contraction** that **lengthens** the joint angle.

**Common movements with Extension:**

1. **Elbow Extension:** Straightening the arm when pushing a door open.
2. **Knee Extension: **Straightening the leg after a squat.
3. **Hip Extension: **Pushing the thigh backward, such as in sprinting or deadlifts.
4. **Neck Extension:** Lifting the chin upward.

<callout type="example">* A weightlifter performing an overhead press – The elbows extend to push the bar up.
* A swimmer pushing off the wall – The knees extend as the legs drive backward.
* A runner sprinting – The rear leg undergoes hip extension as it pushes off the ground.</callout>

<callout type="tip">If flexion decreases a joint angle, extension does the opposite - it increases it!</callout>

<callout type="note">* **Extension does not only happen when moving a limb backward.** 
* It also occurs at the **knee joint when extension moves the lower leg forward**, not backward.</callout>

![Flexion and Extension](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7d623c2ea08412ddf70465c6a6c4fe3e23bbf3ea-3632x1548.jpg)

#### 3. Dorsiflexion


<definition term="Dorsiflexion">Movement of the foot that pulls the toes **upward toward the shin**, decreasing the angle between the foot and lower leg.</definition>

1. Occurs at the **ankle joint**.
2. Involves the **anterior tibialis muscle** pulling the toes up.

**Common movements with Dorsiflexion:**

1. Walking: The foot dorsiflexes when stepping forward.
2. Running: The foot dorsiflexes before the push-off phase.
3. Jumping: The foot dorsiflexes in preparation for takeoff.

<callout type="example">* A sprinter positioning their feet in the starting blocks.
* A basketball player landing from a jump, toes flexed upward.</callout>

<callout type="note">Standing on the heels with the toes lifted is dorsiflexion.</callout>

<callout type="tip">A useful way to remember:

* **Dorsiflexion = toes pointing up** (as if **digging your heels** into the ground).
* **Plantarflexion = toes pointing down** (as if pushing off the ground).</callout>

#### 4. Plantarflexion


<definition term="Plantarflexion">Movement of the foot that points the toes **downward away from the shin**, increasing the angle between the foot and lower leg.</definition>

1. Occurs at the **ankle joint**.
2. Involves contraction of the **calf muscles** (gastrocnemius and soleus).

<callout type="example">* Standing on tiptoes in ballet.
* Pressing the gas pedal in a car.
* Jumping off the ground in sports.

**Examples of Plantarflexion in sports:**

* A basketball player pushing off the ground for a dunk.
* A swimmer pointing their toes during a streamlined glide.
* A soccer player striking a ball with the top of their foot.</callout>

<callout type="tip">Plantarflexion can be remembered as pushing the foot down, like pressing a pedal.</callout>

![Dorsiflexion and plantar flexion](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ef93b71e2adf589966407bd87a432ba69f7e9ad3-1260x744.png)

<callout type="case_study">**Tennis Serve Analysis:**

1. Preparation phase:
  1. Shoulder flexion (sagittal plane)
  2. Trunk rotation (transverse plane)
2. Execution phase:
  1. Shoulder abduction (frontal plane)
  2. Internal rotation (transverse plane)
3. Follow-through:
  1. Multiple plane movements
  2. Complex joint interactions</callout>

## Circumduction


<definition term="Circumduction">**Circumduction** is a **circular movement** of a limb that occurs when the distal end moves in a circular path while the proximal end remains relatively fixed.</definition>

1. It is **not a single movement** but a combination of:
  1. **Flexion**
  2. **Extension**
  3. **Abduction**
  4. **Adduction**
2. Occurs at **multiaxial joints** such as the **shoulder (glenohumeral joint)** and **hip (coxal joint)**.
3. The **joint remains stationary**, but the **limb traces a circular path**.
4. The range of motion depends on **joint mobility and muscle flexibility**.

<callout type="example">* **Arm Circles in Swimming** – The shoulder joint moves through **a full range of motion** in freestyle and backstroke strokes.
* **Softball Windmill Pitch** – The arm moves in a **continuous circular motion** during a pitch.
* **Wrist Circles** – When warming up, moving the wrist in **circular movements** involves circumduction.
* **Gymnast Performing Ribbon Twirls** – The hand moves in **circular paths** while twirling the ribbon.
* A **cricketer bowling** a ball.
* A **tennis player serving** with a large wind-up motion.
* A **soccer player rotating their foot** before kicking a ball.</callout>

![Diagram showing flexion and extension at the shoulder, knee, neck, and vertebral column; abduction, adduction, and circumduction at the shoulder; and medial and lateral rotation of the head, neck, and lower limb.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7b1043d6f3bf5de2a6428e9b4109162fb8c6e7ee-2162x2560.jpg)

<callout type="analogy">Drawing a large circle in the air with the arm while the shoulder remains stationary demonstrates circumduction.</callout>

<callout type="warning">Some students confuse **circumduction with rotation**.

* **Circumduction** = A circular movement using multiple joint actions.
* **Rotation** = Movement **around a single axis** without changing joint position (e.g., turning the head side to side).</callout>

## Protraction and Retraction


### Protraction


<definition term="Protraction">Protraction is the movement of a **body part forward** along a **horizontal plane**.</definition>

1. Protraction involves **anterior movement** (moving forward).
2. It occurs mainly at the **scapulae** and **mandible**.
3. It is associated with **pushing movements**.

<callout type="example">* A boxer **extending their arm forward** in a punch.
* A swimmer **pushing off the wall** at the start of a race.
* A basketball player **reaching for a rebound**.</callout>

<callout type="case_study">Movement Analysis in Basketball:

1. Jump Shot: 
  1. Knee flexion/extension (sagittal)
  2. Shoulder flexion (sagittal)
  3. Wrist flexion (sagittal)
2. Crossover Dribble:
  1. Trunk rotation (transverse)
  2. Hip abduction/adduction (frontal)
3. Rebounding:
  1. Multi-plane movement
  2. Complex coordination</callout>

### Retraction


<definition term="Retraction ">Retraction is the movement of a **body part backward** along a horizontal plane.</definition>

1. **It involves posterior movement** (moving backward).
2. It most commonly occurs at the scapulae and mandible.
3. It is essential for **postural alignment** and **rowing movements**.

<callout type="example">* A rower **pulling the oars back** during a stroke.
* A gymnast holding a **perfect posture** with their shoulders retracted.
* A weightlifter performing a **deadlift**, keeping their **shoulders pulled back**.</callout>

<callout type="tip">**Protraction moves a body part anteriorly, while retraction moves it posteriorly.**</callout>

## Opposition and Reposition


### Opposition


<definition term="Opposition">Opposition is the movement of the **thumb toward the fingertips**, allowing the hand to **grasp, pinch, or manipulate objects**.</definition>

1. It is unique to the **first carpometacarpal joint (thumb joint)**.
2. It is essential for **fine motor skills** and human dexterity.
3. It involves **abduction, medial rotation, and flexion** of the thumb.

<callout type="example">* **Basketball Dribbling** – The player grips the ball using opposition.
* **Rock Climbing** – Climbing requires thumb opposition to hold small holds.
* **Golf Grip** – The thumb helps stabilize the club.</callout>

### Reposition


<definition term="Reposition">Reposition is the movement of the **thumb back to its normal position** after opposition.</definition>

This movement restores the **open hand posture**.

<callout type="example">* **Releasing a Pencil** after writing.
* **Opening the Hand** after gripping a racket.
* **Releasing a Ball** after throwing it.</callout>

<callout type="tip">Opposition = thumb to fingertips,  Reposition = thumb back to normal!</callout>

![Opposition and Reposition. Source: https://orthofixar.com/special-test/hand-wrist-movements/](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/73c9fa53b068beedff2ba5e4a0b0a19c13e5259a-1238x960.png)

<callout type="self_review">* Exam-style prompt: Identify the axis around which a gymnast rotates during a backflip.
* Identify one exercise that involves movement in each of the three planes.
* Cyclist straightening the knee while pushing down on a pedal: identify the movement occurring.
* Plane for all flexion and extension movements: identify the plane.
* Opposition: explain why this movement is essential in humans.
* Shoulder blades squeezed together: identify the movement.
* Opposition and simple thumb flexion: state the difference.</callout>
